source_metadata.title_raw: Jeffrey L. Berk - A-20-008 - January 27, 2020 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 27, 2020


Jeffrey L. Berk
Chief Assistant City Attorney
City of Santa Rosa
100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 8
Santa Rosa, CA 95404

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-20-008

Dear Mr. Berk:

      This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

      Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

      Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

      May Santa Rosa City Councilmembers who own rental properties take part in the decision
of whether to implement a city-wide rental property inspection program, which would require the
payment of inspection fees every five years?

CONCLUSION

      Yes, the City Councilmembers may take part in the decision because the program will
financially affect a significant segment of the public and the effect on the councilmembers'
economic interests is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment of the public.



¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-008
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

At its regular meeting on January 28, 2020, the Santa Rosa City Council is scheduled to consider a proposal for a City-wide rental housing inspection program. The purpose of the program is to provide for periodic proactive inspection of rental units located in the City to ensure habitability and code compliance. Rental units that do not meet basic habitability and code requirements may be referred to Code Enforcement or other mechanisms to ensure that required repairs are made. The program would not change any of the habitability standards or legal requirements that already obligate owners of rental properties to properly maintain their rental units, nor would it increase the penalties for non-compliance. The program would simply provide a new inspection and referral process for enforcement.

The program would apply to all residential rental units within the City, except for: (1) housing units that have been issued a certificate of occupancy within the previous ten years; (2) housing units that are subject to routine periodic inspections under other governmental programs; and (3) owner-occupied housing where the owner rents rooms within the housing unit. Rental housing units would be required to be registered with the City and inspected by the City a minimum of once every five years. Fees would be established by the City Council by resolution. During a follow-up phone call, you clarified that it is estimated a property owner would pay a one-time registration fee of $45.00 and an inspection fee of $172.00 per unit rented. Additional $172 fees could apply if re-inspection or rescheduling is required.

Rental units comprise approximately 47.7% of the total residential units in Santa Rosa. Even with the limited exemption categories, you believe that the total number of residential units subject to the proposed rental housing inspection program will be well over 25% of the total residential units in the City. Rooms rented within owner-occupied residential units are not, and never have been included in the calculation of residential rental units and thus should not affect the analysis.

City Councilmember John Sawyer's spouse owns a single-family residence within the City of Santa Rosa with an attached-in law unit. The two units are rented separately. Councilmember Jack Tibbetts rents out a room in his owner-occupied home.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." Section 87103 explains, "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests. In addition to a public official's personal finances, relevant economic interests include "[a]ny business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more," and "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(a)-(b).)

File No. A-20-008
Page No. 3

Regulation 18701(b) provides the applicable standard for determining the reasonable
foreseeability of a financial effect on a public official's economic interest when that interest is not
explicitly involved in the relevant decision. (Regulation 18701(b).) Under Regulation 18701(b),
"[a] financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the
financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical,
it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary
circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

"The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's
financial interest in a business entity is material if . . . [t]he decision may cause the entity to incur or
avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than . . .
$250,000; or . . . [o]ne percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at
least $2,500." (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).) "The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other
than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision . . . [w]ould impose,
repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel . . . ." (Regulation
18702.2(3).)

"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique
compared to the effect on the significant public segment." (Regulation 18703(a).) A significant segment of
the public includes at least 25 percent of all real property, commercial real property, or residential
real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(2).)

Here, both Councilmembers Sawyer and Tibbetts have business entity and real property
economic interests at issue, given their status as landlords. It is reasonably foreseeable that the
introduction of an inspection program that would apply to rental housing and entail various fees
would have a financial effect on the councilmembers' rental properties. However, analysis of the
Act's materiality standards is unnecessary because, based on the information you have provided,
even with the limited exemption categories, the total number of residential units subject to the
proposed rental housing inspection program will be at least 25% of the total residential real
properties in the City, and therefore the decision of whether to implement the inspection program
will have an effect on a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703(a)-(b).) It does not
appear that either councilmember would be uniquely affected by the decision. (See Regulation
18703(c).) Accordingly, the decision of whether to implement the inspection program would fall
under the "public generally" exception and neither councilmember would be prohibited from taking
part in the decision.

File No. A-20-008
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
